profile.name: John Callahan
roles: Division Chief, Accidents and Investigations Branch, Federal Aviation Administration
Primary FAA custodian of the JAL Flight 1628 evidentiary record (1986)
profile.summary: Former Federal Aviation Administration Division Chief who served as the primary government custodian of the complete evidentiary record for the November 17, 1986 JAL Flight 1628 incident - one of the most corroborated aviation UAP cases in history. A Japan Air Lines Boeing 747 cargo flight en route to Anchorage reported being followed for approximately 400 miles across Alaska by multiple unidentified objects, including one described by the crew as twice the size of an aircraft carrier. Callahan personally compiled the full FAA data package - radar recordings, voice transmissions, and pilot reports - and briefed FAA Administrator Admiral Donald Engen. He subsequently testified that CIA officers attended a follow-up meeting, confiscated all materials, and instructed those present that 'this meeting never happened.' He claims to have retained personal copies of the data, which he brought forward at Steven Greer's 2001 Disclosure Project National Press Club event.
education: Federal aviation management and air traffic control training programs
early_career: Career federal aviation official; rose through the FAA's accident investigation and air traffic safety divisions over several decades

Appointed Division Chief of the Accidents and Investigations Branch in FAA headquarters, Washington D.C. - one of the agency's senior operational roles responsible for investigating significant aviation incidents and managing FAA's evidentiary records

key_events.event: The JAL Flight 1628 incident: on November 17, 1986, Japan Air Lines Boeing 747 cargo flight JL1628, en route from Reykjavik, Iceland to Anchorage, Alaska, reported being followed by multiple unidentified objects over Alaska for approximately 400 miles and 50 minutes. Captain Kenju Terauchi, a veteran pilot with over 10,000 flight hours, reported two small craft and one massive object he described as twice the size of an aircraft carrier. FAA radar at multiple facilities in Alaska confirmed returns consistent with the crew's reports. The encounter drew immediate attention from FAA headquarters and NORAD.

As Division Chief of the Accidents and Investigations Branch, Callahan personally compiled the complete FAA evidentiary record of the JAL 1628 encounter: radar data tapes, voice recordings from the Anchorage Air Route Traffic Control Center, military radar data, and Captain Terauchi's written pilot report. He assembled this package and briefed FAA Administrator Admiral Donald Engen in detail.

A follow-up meeting was convened with FAA officials, USAF representatives, and individuals Callahan identified as CIA officers. According to Callahan's account, the CIA officers took possession of the complete evidentiary package - all copies known to the FAA - and instructed all personnel present that 'this meeting never happened' and that they were not to discuss the incident. Callahan states that he retained personal copies of key materials that were not surrendered.

FAA Administrator Admiral Donald Engen publicly acknowledged the JAL 1628 incident at a press conference, confirming that something had been tracked on FAA radar alongside the Japan Air Lines flight. This represents the FAA's highest-level public confirmation of the incident. However, an FAA spokesman subsequently offered the explanation that the radar returns may have been a 'split return' from Jupiter and Mars - an explanation widely criticized as technically inadequate by aviation and astronomy experts.

Testified at Steven Greer's Disclosure Project National Press Club event in Washington D.C., providing the most detailed public account of the JAL 1628 incident and the alleged CIA data confiscation. Presented his account alongside over 20 other former military and government witnesses calling for UAP transparency. His testimony is archived in the Disclosure Project record.

His FAA credentials and the JAL 1628 incident were documented and confirmed by The UFO Chronicles, which independently verified his Division Chief role. The case and his account are cited in Leslie Kean's 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record' (2010), which includes the testimony of Captain Terauchi as a primary witness.

claims.claim: CIA officers attended a FAA follow-up meeting on the JAL 1628 incident, confiscated all evidentiary materials, and instructed attendees that 'this meeting never happened.'
claims.notes: This claim rests entirely on Callahan's own testimony. No corroborating witness from the meeting has come forward publicly. The CIA has not acknowledged the meeting or any involvement in the JAL 1628 investigation. The FAA's contemporaneous handling of the incident - including the distribution of radar data and the subsequent offer of the technically dubious 'Jupiter and Mars' explanation - is consistent with an institutional effort to minimize the incident, but does not directly confirm CIA involvement or data confiscation. Callahan's FAA credentials and Division Chief role have been independently verified.

claims.claim: The objects tracked by FAA radar and reported by JAL 1628's crew were genuine anomalous aerial phenomena with no conventional explanation.
claims.notes: The FAA's own investigation confirmed radar returns alongside JL1628 that were inconsistent with conventional traffic. FAA Administrator Engen publicly acknowledged the radar contacts. Captain Terauchi was a veteran pilot with over 10,000 hours and no prior UAP history. The 50-minute duration, multi-facility radar confirmation, and crew's detailed descriptions make this one of the better-documented aviation UAP cases on record. The official 'Jupiter and Mars' explanation has been technically criticized by aviation experts and astronomers. However, natural radar anomalies (atmospheric ducting, temperature inversions) and psychological factors in long-duration nighttime flight have not been definitively ruled out as contributing factors.

supporting: Career federal aviation official whose Division Chief role at FAA headquarters has been independently confirmed

The JAL 1628 incident itself is extensively documented in contemporaneous FAA records, multiple radar facilities' data, and press coverage (Los Angeles Times, New York Times, aviation media) dating from 1986-1987
FAA Administrator Admiral Donald Engen publicly acknowledged the incident at a press conference, providing the agency's senior-level confirmation that something was tracked on FAA radar
Captain Kenju Terauchi was a veteran commercial pilot with over 10,000 flight hours and no prior UAP history, lending his account unusual credibility among aviation witnesses
The 50-minute duration across approximately 400 miles and multi-facility radar confirmation make JAL 1628 among the most corroborated aviation UAP cases in the record
The FAA's offered explanation ('Jupiter and Mars split return') was publicly criticized as technically inadequate by aviation experts and astronomers at the time of its release
contradicting: The CIA confiscation claim rests entirely on Callahan's testimony; no corroborating witness has come forward
The Disclosure Project framing of his testimony - assembled by Steven Greer - is not an ideal credibility context, as Greer's maximalist positions have been challenged by other UAP researchers
Japan Air Lines corporately reassigned Captain Terauchi after the incident, suggesting the airline's own institutional discomfort with the account rather than full endorsement
The FAA's ROTOR radar data for the encounter has never been made fully public, making independent analysis of the radar contacts difficult
Natural radar anomalies in the Alaska region cannot be definitively excluded without access to the full technical data record

associated_people.name: Dr. Steven M. Greer
associated_people.relationship: Greer provided the platform for Callahan's most prominent public testimony at the 2001 Disclosure Project National Press Club event. Callahan's account of the JAL 1628 incident and CIA confiscation is documented in the Disclosure Project archive.

associated_people.name: Leslie Kean
associated_people.relationship: Kean's 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record' (2010) documents the JAL 1628 case in detail, including the primary testimony of Captain Terauchi. Callahan's account and the FAA evidentiary record form part of the case's documented history in Kean's research.

disclosures.title: Disclosure Project National Press Club Testimony
disclosures.notes: Callahan's primary public testimony on the JAL 1628 incident and the alleged CIA data confiscation. Presented alongside 20+ former military and government witnesses at Greer's Disclosure Project event. Archived in the Disclosure Project record.

career_connections.relationship: As FAA Division Chief, Callahan was the primary government custodian of the complete evidentiary record for the JAL 1628 incident - the most corroborated aviation UAP case of the 1980s - compiling radar data, voice recordings, and pilot reports and briefing FAA Administrator Admiral Engen.

Greer provided the platform for Callahan's most prominent public testimony at the 2001 Disclosure Project NPC event, where Callahan presented his account of the JAL 1628 incident and the alleged CIA data confiscation.

Kean documented the JAL 1628 case extensively in her 2010 book 'UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record,' drawing on the FAA evidentiary record and Captain Terauchi's testimony that Callahan helped bring to public attention.
